title: Perez v. Estrada — Live Radio-TV Coverage, Bane or Boon?
primary_topics: Perez v. Estrada live media coverage prohibition
due process vs press freedom balancing
Panganiban Dissent advocating single-fixed-camera technology solution
sub_topics: October 23, 1991 absolute prohibition on live radio-TV coverage of court proceedings
Estes v. Texas (1965, US Supreme Court 5-4)
Kapisanan ng mga Brodkaster ng Pilipinas (KBP) March 13, 2001 letter
Secretary of Justice Hernando Perez Petition
Davide, Bellosillo, Melo, Puno, Panganiban, Quisumbing dissenters
Puno's standards for trial-judge discretion + 'Rip Van Winkles' warning
National Museum / Records Management and Archives Office
Rule 119 Section 21 Revised Rules of Criminal Procedure

stances.claim: (Majority per Vitug) Live television coverage of judicial proceedings involves 'an inherent denial of the due process rights' of a criminal defendant — drawing from Estes v. Texas (US), the four potential prejudice areas (jury, witnesses, trial judge, defendant) outweigh press-freedom interests; 'the right of the accused must be preferred to win.'

(Majority) 'A public trial is not synonymous with publicized trial; it only implies that the court doors must be open to those who wish to come, sit in the available seats, conduct themselves with decorum and observe the trial process.' A courtroom needs 'enough facilities for a reasonable number of the public to observe' — not infinite remote viewers.

(Majority) 'A trial is not a free trade of ideas. Nor is a competing market of thoughts, the known test of truth in a courtroom' — quoting Frankfurter dissent in Bridges v. California to distinguish judicial from democratic-deliberative arenas.

(CJ's Dissent) 'It is technologically possible to uphold the right of the people to public information without violating the right of the accused to due process and without impeding the orderly administration of justice' — the ban was first promulgated in 1991, but the 2001 'new world order brought about by the information highway' has made the 1991 reasons 'obsolete.'

(CJ's Dissent) Solution: 'a single fixed camera under the control of the court' in the rear of the courtroom, locked to wide angle, no other cameras or lights — output flashed on TV monitors/projection screens; broadcasters connect via distribution amplifiers from outside the courtroom; viewers see only what a courtroom spectator would see.

(CJ's Dissent) Recipients of TV signals outside the courtroom 'cannot interfere in or prejudice the fair and orderly administration of justice, for the simple reason that they are outside the vicinity of the courtroom' — they cannot be the 'hooting throng' the IBP scorned and feared.
stances.rhetorical_move: spatial-isolation framing to defeat hooting-throng objection

stances.claim: (CJ's Dissent Epilogue) 'The Court, by its Decision today, has turned its back and refused to march in cadence with the music created by the chimes of changing technology' — to keep public trust and esteem, transparency and accountability are 'cornerstones of this Court's avowed policies as it celebrated its centenary on June 11, 2001.'
(CJ's Dissent close) 'Let the light of truth be beamed to all the people through the advances of science and technology in the 21st century!'

(September 13, 2001 compromise) Audio-visual recording for documentary purposes is ordered with strict conditions — inconspicuously installed cameras, no live broadcast until Sandiganbayan decision, master film deposited at National Museum and Records Management and Archives Office.

notable_anecdotes: KBP, Hernando Perez, Cesar Sarino, Senator Compañero Cayetano, and Atty. Ricardo Romulo successively pushing for live coverage of Estrada plunder case
Puno's Dissent warning of 'Rip Van Winkles in this age of electronic media — We' — Puno's sharp parting line

The September 13, 2001 compromise: 8-7 vote ordering audio-visual recording for documentary purposes, master film deposited at National Museum and Records Management and Archives Office, no live broadcast until Sandiganbayan decision
Mendoza and Reyes — who had voted against live coverage — joining CJ's six dissenters to form the 8 in favor of documentary recording
